body_txt: Many stories have a Happy Ending . Sometimes, the characters have to earn it . Sometimes, a random last-minute swerve saves the day . And sometimes, things are dark, but ultimately look like they'll work out . But sometimes, there is no happiness in the ending. Sometimes, there's a Downer Ending; an ending where the curtain falls on an upsetting, depressing, or bleak scene. While there might be a small bit of light in the darkness , the story has still come to a close with everything in a worse place than where it started. Simply put, it's where a work has an ending that's designed to make the audience sad. Downer Endings typically come in two flavors:   Downer endings have effectively driven home an episode's Aesop . It's the only effective way to deliver the message for some issues. Sometimes, a downer ending can conclude the character arcs in a very satisfying way, despite (or because of) being dark, if the characters are Villain Protagonists . People feel that they got what was coming to them (unless the ending is a downer because the villain protagonists win ). This is not an absolute rule, however, and there are cases where the audience can have enough sympathy and pity for the characters in question, especially if they have sympathetic qualities and poignant moments that can lead more into an Alas, Poor Villain perspective, especially when the character in question was once a hero . Other times, it can be overused to the point it feels like a Mandatory Twist Ending . If it comes out of nowhere or results from an Ass Pull , it may result from the writers summoning Diabolus ex Machina . If enough people consider it fundamentally unjust, it may acquire the Fanon Discontinuity and Snicket Warning Labels . The "Everybody Dies" Ending , Shoot the Shaggy Dog , and The Bad Guy Wins are particularly cruel variations of this. A Legally Savvy Crook getting away with heinous crimes can also serve. A Prequel may require one or both; the Happy Ending was Doomed by Canon . This can result if the protagonist is doomed since the beginning and dies at the end , except when they get a cure or miracle for this . Or it can also be the result of failing to reach an Overly Ambitious Goal . If the ending mixes elements of happiness and a Downer Ending, then it's a Bittersweet Ending . If the whole adventure leading up to it wasn't meaningful, it's Shoot the Shaggy Dog . The Cliffhanger is often a Downer Ending, but one that's implicitly not final; feel free to tune in next time to see how they get this one. The Bolivian Army Ending is usually a Downer Ending, though it ends ambiguously enough to leave some room for hope. If only the main characters get this, see Only the Leads Get a Downer Ending . See also Gainax Ending , Last Breath Bullet , Crapsack World , Yank the Dog's Chain , It's a Wonderful Failure , and It All Ends in Tears . Occasionally, part of a Tragedy or an Utsuge . Sometimes a Foregone Conclusion , either because of a historical setting or if the work is a Prequel . The hero may or may not die . Some video games feature this as a Non-Standard Game Over . It's not uncommon for at least one of them to be a total bummer in games with Multiple Endings . However, it's very unusual (and occasionally downright spiteful) for all of them to be downers. Realization of an Inferred Holocaust may turn even the happiest of "happy endings" into one. When it's caused by unfinished work, it's an Accidental Downer Ending . Compare Downer Beginning . Contrast Happy Ending . As a last comment, Downer Endings are sometimes argued to make a story pointless. After all, what's the point of getting invested in the characters and setting if it's all going to get bulldozed by the end anyways? In reality, these kinds of endings can be essential to reflecting real life. Things don't always work out in real life, in spite of the best actions one can take, so seeing that reflected in a work can be a powerful way to build empathy with the characters. As well, sometimes we can learn what not to do to earn an ending like this, so that we as humans don't end up making the same mistakes over and over again.
